coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence ‘‘turn-on’’ probe for the selective detection of water in organic solvents based on functionalized mesoporous silica
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Jing
Zhao, Qian
Zhang, Hao
Liu, Hai-Bo
Wang, Jing
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescence ‘‘turn-on’’ probe, namely, TTA-SBA-15, for the selective detection of water (H2O) was designed by grafting terthiophene fluorophore onto ethylenediamine functionalized mesoporous SBA-15 silica. The maximum fluorescence emission peak of TTA-SBA-15 ranged from 462 nm (toluene) to 525 nm (methanol) in various organic solvents. No fluorescence was observed in H2O due to the donor-excited photoinduced electron transfer mechanism, in which terthiophene acted as the donor and the amino group acted as the acceptor. Upon adding trace amounts of H2O into the TTA-SBA-15 suspensions dispersed in various organic solvents, TTA-SBA-15 was successfully applied as a ‘‘turn-on’’ fluorescent probe for the quantitative determination of trace H2O in organic solvents with high sensitivity and low detection limit. To demonstrate the selective detection mechanism of TTA-SBA-15 for H2O, the fluorescent spectra of two control materials (TT-SBA-15 and PyA-SBA-15) were also investigated in H2O and various organic solvents. The experimental results indicated that the terthiophene fluorophore and amine functional group on TTA-SBA-15 contributed to the H2O selectivity, highlighting the structure-activity relationships in developing organic functionalized mesoporous silica for potential applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mesoporous silica
Organic function
Structure-activity relationship
Selective detection
Trace H2O

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedures of water sensing in organic solvents
Determination of H2O in organic solvents

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (Grant No. 21966006).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical structures (a) and solid-state photos of TTA-SBA-15, TT-SBA-15, and PyA-SBA-15 under day light (b) and 365 nm UV light irradiation (c).
SXRD patterns (a), TEM image (b), and N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of TTA-SBA-15 (c).
FT-IR spectra of SBA-15 (a), AAPTMS-SBA-15 (b), and TTA-SBA-15 (c).
Fluorescence spectra of (a) TTA-SBA-15 (0.05 g L−1) and (b) TT-SBA-15 (0.05 g/L) in various solvents. The photos were taken under 365 nm UV light irradiation.
Fluorescence spectra of TTA-SBA-15 (0.05 g L−1) with different water content in (a) THF, (b) AN, (c) DMF, (d) DMSO, (e) EtOH, and (f) MeOH. The photos were taken under 365 nm UV light irradiation.
Fluorescence spectra of TTA-SBA-15 (0.05 g L−1) for determination of trace H2O in (a) THF, (b) CH3CN, (c) DMF, (d) DMSO, (e) EtOH, and (f) MeOH.
Fluorescence intensity of TTA-SBA-15 versus the content of H2O in (a) THF, (b) CH3CN, (c) DMF, (d) DMSO, (e) EtOH, and (f) MeOH.
Schematic of the preparation procedure for TTA-SBA-15.
Schematic of the preparation procedure for PyA-SBA-15.
Fluorescence mechanism in terms of molecular orbitals of the fluorophore (TT) and the acceptor (NN).
Summary of the water detection performance of TTA-SBA-15.
Comparison of the sensing features of TTA-SBA-15 with other examples.

row.entry: THF, AN, DMF, DMSO, EtOH, MeOH

head.ce:title: Fluorescence ‘‘turn-on’’ probe for the selective detection of water in organic solvents based on functionalized mesoporous silica

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescence ‘‘turn-on’’ probe (TTA-SBA-15) for the selective detection of water (H2O) was designed, by grafting terthiophene fluorophore onto ethylenediamine functionalized mesoporous SBA-15 silica. Two control materials (TT-SBA-15 and PyA-SBA-15) were also investigated, and the results demonstrated that the terthiophene fluorophore and amine functional group on TTA-SBA-15 contributed to the H2O selectivity.

A fluorescence ‘‘turn-on’’ probe, namely, TTA-SBA-15, for the selective detection of water (H2O) was designed by grafting terthiophene fluorophore onto ethylenediamine functionalized mesoporous SBA-15 silica. The maximum fluorescence emission peak of TTA-SBA-15 ranged from 462 nm (toluene) to 525 nm (methanol) in various organic solvents. No fluorescence was observed in H2O due to the donor-excited photoinduced electron transfer mechanism, in which terthiophene acted as the donor and the amino group acted as the acceptor. Upon adding trace amounts of H2O into the TTA-SBA-15 suspensions dispersed in various organic solvents, TTA-SBA-15 was successfully applied as a ‘‘turn-on’’ fluorescent probe for the quantitative determination of trace H2O in organic solvents with high sensitivity and low detection limit. To demonstrate the selective detection mechanism of TTA-SBA-15 for H2O, the fluorescent spectra of two control materials (TT-SBA-15 and PyA-SBA-15) were also investigated in H2O and various organic solvents. The experimental results indicated that the terthiophene fluorophore and amine functional group on TTA-SBA-15 contributed to the H2O selectivity, highlighting the structure-activity relationships in developing organic functionalized mesoporous silica for potential applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Water (H2O) is one of the most basic and important chemicals for life. However, it is also the most common impurity in organic solvents, which is often harmful to many manufacturing processes [1–3]. Trace water could inhibit the reaction by quenching the reaction site, deactivating and degrading the catalyst, resulting in reduced product yield. In severe cases, it can cause fires and explosions [4–5]. Therefore, the qualitative and quantitative analysis of water is of great significance in chemical reactions and industrial applications. Karl Fischer titration is the most widely used method for detecting trace water in the laboratory. However, this method requires strict sample preparation and involves complex operation [6]. Other detection methods such as gas chromatography [7], electrochemistry [8–9], and near-infrared absorption spectroscopy [10] lack portability, and are time-consuming and expensive. To overcome these limitations, fluorescent H2O probes are considered as a potential alternative due to their simple operation; fast response; high sensitivity and selectivity; and cost-effective, non-invasive, and in situ detection [11–13]. Therefore, much effort has been devoted to develop fluorescent probes for water detection [14–15].

Organic molecule-based fluorescent water probes have been widely reported [16–17], which have the advantages of low cost, high sensitivity, and fast response [18–19]. However, these systems are often accompanied by poor water solubility, poor stability, and difficult recovery. To overcome these disadvantages, fluorescent nanomaterials such as Cu nanoclusters [20], covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [21], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [22], and carbon dots [23] have been exploited as water probes. These nanomaterials have excellent properties including simple synthesis, good stability, and recyclability and reusability. However, some detection methods reported in literature had a “turn-off” response [13,20], which was easily affected by the detection background. Moreover, most studies could only quantitatively detect trace H2O in organic solvents, but could not selectively recognize H2O from various solvents. Therefore, fluorescent probes with high H2O selectivity need to be developed.

Among various nanomaterials, mesoporous silicas exhibit fascinating features such as highly ordered pore structure, large specific surface area, high stability, and good biocompatibility [24–25]. In particular, the abundance of hydroxyl groups on its surface makes mesoporous silica such as SBA-15 an ideal supporting matrix for optical sensitive indicators and selective organic functional groups (receptors), which could be conveniently immobilized in the physical space of the mesoporous framework by post-modification [26–28]. These heterostructures can maintain the excellent structural features of mesoporous silica and the optical properties of the functional units simultaneously, which shows the advantages of photostability, design flexibility, and multifunctionality. Thus, SBA-15 should be an ideal material to be used as a substrate for photochemical probes. The physical properties of mesoporous silica could be retained, and sensing capability could be achieved by grafting indicators and receptors onto SBA-15. At present, functionalized mesoporous silica has been widely used in the detection of various analytes, such as metal ions [24,26] and anions [30]. However, few studies have used functionalized mesoporous silica in the selective detection of H2O.

In the present work, we developed an organic functional mesoporous silica, namely, TTA-SBA-15, by attaching a terthiophene derivative and an NH-containing organic group onto SBA-15 to selectively detect H2O in organic solvents. The fluorescence properties of TTA-SBA-15 in H2O were significantly different from those in organic solvents. To understand the mechanism behind the selective detection of H2O, two control materials (TT-SBA-15 and PyA-SBA-15) were prepared, and their fluorescence properties in H2O and organic solvents were also investigated, revealing the structure-activity relationships. The sensitive detection of H2O in organic solvents was demonstrated by titrating trace amounts of H2O into TTA-SBA-15 dispersed in organic solvents.

SBA-15 was obtained from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. (China) and used as received. N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (AAPTMS), 2,2′:5′,2′'-terthiophene-5-carbaldehyde (TT) and 1-pyrenecarboxaldehyde were purchased form Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai). The organic reagents including acetonitrile (AN), ethanol (EtOH), methanol (MeOH), dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), chloroform (CHCl3), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), acetone, ethyl acetate (EA), toluene and tetrahydrofuran (THF) were purchased from commercial suppliers. The small angle X-ray powder diffraction (SXRD) pattern was obtained using smartlab9K diffractometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were performed on JEOL JEM-2100 F microscope operated at 300 kV. The thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted on a Netzsch STA449-F5 TAQ600 instrument at a 10 °C min−1 heating rate. Micrometrics ASAP 2460 instrument was used to measure the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) were recorded on Varian 660-IR (ATR) FT-IR spectrophotometer. Fluorescence studies were done using 960 MC spectroscopy (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd., China), with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

As shown in Scheme 1 , 0.5 g SBA-15 was added to 30 mL anhydrous toluene and stirred for 30 min to make it evenly dispersed, then 1.0 mL AAPTMS was dissolved in the mixture, followed by refluxing for 12 h at 110 ℃. The obtained AAPTMS-SBA-15 was centrifugally separated, washed with chloroform (5 × 20 mL) and dried at 80 ℃ for 12 h. Whereafter, 0.4 g AAPTMS-SBA-15 was stirred in 30 mL toluene for 20 min, 1.0 mmol (0.2724 g) of TT was then added and refluxed at 110 ℃ for 24 h. The product was cleaned with chloroform and centrifuged to remove excess TT. TTA-SBA-15 was obtained after drying at 80 ℃. The TGA curves of TTA-SBA-15 indicated an obvious weight loss (Fig. S1), the amount of functional group in SBA-15 was approximately 0.53 mmol g−1.

As shown in Scheme 2 , the preparation method of PyA-SBA-15 was similar to TTA-SBA-15, except that the TT was replaced with pyrene formaldehyde. According to the TGA data, approximately 0.55 mmol/g of 1-pyrenecarboxaldehyde was anchored to the inner walls of PyA–SBA–15 (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures of water sensing in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, for H2O sensing experiments, 5 mg of TTA-SBA-15 was dispersed in 10.0 mL of THF, followed by ultrasonication for 15 min to obtain 0.5 g L−1 of stabilized stock suspensions. Then, 0.5 mL of the prepared TTA-SBA-15 stock suspensions was added into different volumes of water using a transfer pipette. Subsequently, THF was added to keep the final volume of water/THF at 5.0 mL. Ultrasound was applied to these mixtures to maintain uniform dispersion during the experiment. The sensing of water in AN, DMF, DMSO, EtOH, and MeOH was performed in a similar way.

Fig. 1 shows the chemical structures and the corresponding solid-state photographs of TTA-SBA-15, TT-SBA-15, and PyA-SBA-15. TTA-SBA-15 is coupled by a terthiophene-based derivative (i.e., TT) and ethylenediamine-functionalized mesoporous SBA-15 silica. TT-SBA-15 has the same fluorophore as TTA-SBA-15 but has one less NH group on the organic functions. PyA-SBA-15 is derived from the coupling of a pyrene fluorophore and ethylenediamine-functionalized mesoporous SBA-15 silica. All three samples were yellow under daylight. Under a UV lamp, TTA-SBA-15 and TT-SBA-15 showed bright yellow color, whereas PyA-SBA-15 showed yellow-green color.

The preparation and characterization of TT-SBA-15 had been described in our previous work [31]. The SXRD patterns of TTA-SBA-15 and PyA-SBA-15 are demonstrated in Fig. 2 (a) and Fig. S3 (a). Three distinct reflections are attributed to the (100), (110), and (200) planes, which reveal the hexagonal structure of SBA-15 [29]. The TEM images of TTA-SBA-15 (Fig. 2b) and PyA-SBA-15 (Fig. S3b) clearly displayed uniform and ordered parallel channel structure, indicating that the functionalized mesoporous silica material still maintains the ordered parallel channel structure [30]. The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of TTA-SBA-15 (Fig. 2c) are type IV, with clear H1 hysteresis loop at high relative pressure, which is the typical property of mesoporous materials [32] (Table S1). The isotherms further indicated that TT grafting was successful and the mesoporous structure of SBA-15 was well preserved.

The FT-IR spectra of SBA-15, AAPTMS-SBA-15, and TTA-SBA-15 are shown in Fig. 3. All samples included a broad O–H stretching band of Si–OH groups around 3440 cm−1 and characteristic Si–O–Si bands at 1083, 809, and 462 cm−1, which are consistent with the SBA-15 silica framework [33]. The stretching vibrations of unsaturated hydrocarbon C–H group at 3070 cm−1, saturated hydrocarbon C–H group at 2933 and 2885 cm−1, C=N group at 1630 cm−1, C=C group of thiophene at 1470 and 1450 cm−1, and C–S group at 692 cm−1, indicated that TTA-SBA-15 had been successfully constructed. The FT-IR spectra of PyA-SBA-15 also verified the successful preparation of PyA-SBA-15 (Fig. S4).

The fluorescence spectra of TTA-SBA-15, TT-SBA-15, and PyA-SBA-15 were investigated in various organic solvents and H2O (Fig. 4 , Fig. S5 and Table S2). TTA-SBA-15 showed different fluorescence emission in different solvents. The maximum fluorescence emission peak of TTA-SBA-15 was above 500 nm in EtOH, MeOH, and DMSO and ranged from 462 nm to 493 nm in other organic solvents. However, no emission was observed in H2O. Thus, the photophysical properties of TTA-SBA-15 in H2O were distinct from those of organic solvents, demonstrating the selectivity of TTA-SBA-15 toward H2O.

As a control, TT-SBA-15, which has the same fluorophore as TTA-SBA-15, displayed similar photophysical properties in various organic solvents (Fig. 4b and Table S2). Interestingly, the fluorescence of TTA-SBA-15 and TT-SBA-15 showed obvious differences in aqueous solution. The maximum fluorescence emission band of TT-SBA-15 appeared at 454 nm. Thus, TT-SBA-15 did not possess desirable ability to distinguish between organic solvents and water. As another control, PyA-SBA-15 possessed pyrene fluorophore, which was different from TTA-SBA-15. Other organic groups were the same as TTA-SBA-15. The position and morphology of the fluorescence emission peaks of PyA-SBA-15 were almost the same in various organic solvents. Moreover, PyA-SBA-15 could not distinguish between organic solvents and water by comparing the fluorescence spectra and fluorescence color under UV lamp (Fig. S5). The results of these controlled experiments indicated that the terthiophene fluorophore and amino group on TTA-SBA-15 contributed to the H2O selectivity [34–35].

The rich fluorescence characteristic phenomenon of TTA-SBA-15 and TT-SBA-15 in organic solvents may be attributed to the presence of thiophene fluorophore. Moreover, the introduction of –NH group could be fundamental for the different fluorescence properties of TTA-SBA-15 from TT-SBA-15 in aqueous solution. Thus, theoretical calculations were carried out to confirm this conjecture. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels of thiophene fluorophore (TT) and the acceptor (NN-H) were calculated, and the electron distributions were displayed to gain a deeper understanding of the quenching mechanism of TTA-SBA-15 toward H2O. The LUMO of TT was lower than that of NN, which inhibited the electron transfer in organic solvents (Fig. S6). Consequently, TTA-SBA-15 was fluorescent in these organic solvents. When TTA-SBA-15 was dispersed in aqueous solution, the NH group was protonated. The relevant LUMO energy level of TT became higher than that of NN-H (Fig. S6). Thus, the donor-excited photoinduced electron transfer (d-PET) [36] occurred from the excited thiophene fluorophore to the electron acceptor of NH-H (Scheme 3 ), resulting in the fluorescence quenching of TTA-SBA-15, in which the electron was prevented to come back to the ground state by the radiative process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of H2O in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: The above results revealed that the fluorescence emission properties of TTA-SBA-15 in H2O were distinct from those of organic solvents, such as THF, CH3CN, DMF, DMSO, EtOH, and MeOH. Therefore, the presence of H2O in these solvents would result in fluorescence alteration. Then, the emission behavior of TTA-SBA-15 in solvent–water mixtures was investigated using different water fractions. The recorded photographs and spectra are displayed in Fig. 5 . Take the photophysical properties of TTA-SB-15 in a THF–water mixture as an example (Fig. 5a). With increasing water fraction from 0% to 70%, the emission intensity of TTA-SBA-15 significantly increased. This phenomenon is attributed to aggregation-induced emission (AIE). A further increase of the water fraction above 80% led to a decrease of the emission intensity. However, at higher water contents (90%), the solution had almost no fluorescence emission, indicating that d-PET dominates over AIE. Another important finding is that the AIE effect also induced gradual red-shift in the emission wavelength from 476 nm to 548 nm. Accordingly, the emission color changed from blue to yellow with the increase of water contents. A similar phenomenon has been observed in other solvents. Similar to THF, the fluorescence increased first, and then decreased with the increase of water content in other organic solvents. The fluorescence enhancement–reduction phenomenon had previously been observed in some compounds with AIE properties [37–38].

The fluorescence spectra of TT-SBA-15 with different water content in organic solvents, such as DMF, DMSO, EtOH, and MeOH, were also investigated. With increasing water content, the fluorescence intensity of TT-SBA-15 increased first and then decreased (Fig. S7). However, at a higher water content (90%), the emission peak of TT-SBA-15 in the mixed solution appeared at about 450 nm, and the solution was blue-purple under ultraviolet light, which was different from the fluorescence response of TTA-SBA-15 to water in organic solvents, further confirming that the d-PET mechanism contributed to the detection of water by TTA-SBA-15.

As shown in Fig. 5 and S8, when the H2O fractions increased from 0% to 10.0%, remarkable spectral changes in emission wavelength and intensity were found in THF, CH3CN, DMF, DMSO, EtOH, and MeOH. Then, we attempted to quantitatively detect trace amounts of H2O in these six organic solvents. Upon increasing the H2O content to 10.0%, the turn-on fluorescence response of TTA-SBA-15 was obviously observed. The emission intensity of TTA-SBA-15 was gradually enhanced and slightly red-shifted in the six different solvent solutions (Fig. 6 ), which could be due to the AIE phenomenon. The fluorescent signals in THF (F498nm), AN (F518nm), DMF (F488nm), DMSO (F506nm), EtOH (F524nm), and MeOH (F530nm) versus the concentration of H2O displayed favorable linear relationships in H2O ranges of 0%–0.6%, 0%–2.0%, 0%–1.0%, 0%–2.0%, 0%–1.0%, and 0%–8.0%, respectively (Fig. 7 ). According to the equation
L O D
=
3 σ / k
, where σ is the standard deviation of blank solutions of TTA-SBA-15 measured by 10 times and k is the slope of fluorescence intensity versus [H2O] plot, the theoretical detection limit of TTA-SBA-15 for trace water in different solvents could be evaluated from the fluorescence titration curve. The corresponding detection limit of H2O was calculated to be 0.014, 0.02, 0.021, 0.096, 0.019, and 0.068 for THF, AN, DMF, DMSO, EtOH, and MeOH, respectively (Table 1 ). As the absolute emission intensity will depend on a variety of parameters, the changes in fluorescence intensity (I/I0) versus the content of H2O in different organic solvent were calculated to calibrate the detection system. The data of the relative information (I/I0) was shown in Fig. S9, which was consistent with the results calculated from the absolute emission intensity.

The performance of TTA-SBA-15 was compared with that of various fluorescent H2O probes. As shown in Table 2 , compared with most of the reported H2O probes, TTA-SBA-15 displayed several promising properties: (1) it had excellent H2O selectivity, (2) it had a “turn-on” detection mode that was superior to “turn-off,” and (3) trace water could be detected in various organic solvents.

In summary, we prepared an organic–inorganic hybrid mesoporous material (i.e., TTA-SBA-15) and demonstrated its applicability as a fluorescent H2O probe. The fluorescent signals of TTA-SBA-15 could distinguish H2O from various organic solvents. The high H2O selectivity of TTA-SBA-15 was due to the d-PET effect. TTA-SBA-15 showed obvious fluorescence enhancement upon exposure to trace H2O in different organic solvents, which was attributed to the AIE mechanism. TTA-SBA-15 exhibited desirable sensing capacity for H2O, such as excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, and low detection limit, thus indicating that it can be used for detecting H2O in various organic solvents. The proposed method provides an idea for sensing applications by combining mesoporous silica materials with different organic functional groups and fluorophores.

Jing Huang: Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Qian Zhao: Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Investigation. Hao Zhang: Software. Hai-Bo Liu: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Jing Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Visualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (Grant No. 21966006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122099.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Turn-on luminescence sensing and real-time detection of traces of water in organic solvents by a flexible metal-organic framework
Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.
